subject: Company Law
module_title: Introduction to Company Law and Incorporation

questions.question: Under section 31 CA 2014, the constitution of a company binds:

options.B: The company and its members to the same extent as a signed contract
options.C: The directors and the company only
options.D: The members inter se but not the company

questions.explanation: Section 31 CA 2014 provides that the constitution binds the company and each member to the same extent as if they had each signed and sealed the constitution. This creates a statutory contract between the company and each member, and between members inter se.

questions.question: In Hickman v Kent or Romney Marsh Sheepbreeders Association [1915], the court held that:

options.A: A member can enforce any provision of the articles against the company
options.B: A member can only enforce the articles against the company in their capacity as a member
options.C: The articles bind the company but not outsiders
options.D: An outsider named in the articles can enforce them as a third-party beneficiary

questions.explanation: Hickman v Kent [1915] 1 Ch 881 established the rule that a member may only enforce the articles in their capacity as a member — not to enforce a right given to them in some other capacity (such as an outsider right or a right as a solicitor or director).

questions.question: In Eley v Positive Government Security Life Assurance Co [1876], the plaintiff sought to enforce a provision in the articles that appointed him as the company's solicitor. The court held:

options.A: He could enforce the clause as a member of the company
options.B: He could not enforce the clause as it was an outsider right and the articles do not give rights to outsiders in their non-member capacity
options.C: He was entitled to damages from the company for breach of contract
options.D: The clause was void as it was a restraint of trade

questions.explanation: In Eley v Positive Government Security Life Assurance Co [1876] 1 ExD 88, the Court of Appeal held that the plaintiff, although a shareholder, could not enforce the articles as a contract for his employment as solicitor — the provision conferred a right on him in his capacity as an outsider, not as a member.

questions.question: In Rayfield v Hands [1960], the court enforced a provision in the articles requiring directors to purchase the shares of any member wishing to sell. This was enforced as:

options.A: An outsider right granted by the articles to the director
options.B: A member-to-member obligation, enforceable because the directors were also members
options.C: A court-ordered specific performance of a directors' duty
options.D: A statutory obligation under the Companies Act 1948

questions.explanation: In Rayfield v Hands [1960] Ch 1, Vaisey J held that the obligation was enforceable because the directors were also members and the articles created a contractual obligation between the member qua member and the directors qua members.

questions.question: An ‘outsider right’ in the context of section 31 CA 2014 refers to:

options.A: A right conferred by the constitution on a third party who is not a member
options.B: A right conferred on a person in a capacity other than as a member (e.g., as solicitor, auditor, or director)
options.C: A right that cannot be enforced by either the company or the members
options.D: A foreign shareholder's right to vote

questions.explanation: An outsider right is a right conferred by the constitution on a person in a capacity other than as a member — for example, as solicitor, director, or officer. Such rights are not enforceable through the S31 statutory contract; only rights in the capacity of member are enforceable.

questions.question: In Bamford v Bamford [1970], the court held that:

options.A: A director who breaches fiduciary duty cannot be ratified by shareholders
options.B: A breach of duty by directors in allotting shares can be ratified by the members in general meeting by ordinary resolution
options.C: A special resolution is required to ratify a breach of director duty
options.D: The company is automatically wound up where a director breaches duty

questions.explanation: In Bamford v Bamford [1970] Ch 212, the Court of Appeal confirmed that shareholders can ratify a voidable act of the directors by ordinary resolution passed at a general meeting — the ratification relates back and validates the act from the outset.

questions.question: The S31 statutory contract differs from an ordinary contract in that:

options.A: It can only be enforced by the company against members, not by members against the company
options.B: It does not require offer and acceptance to bind the parties
options.C: It is enforced by statute and arises automatically on registration — no separate signed document is required
options.D: Consideration is not required for it to be binding

questions.explanation: Unlike an ordinary contract, the S31 statutory contract arises by operation of statute on registration of the constitution. No separate signatures or consideration are required — all members are bound automatically and simultaneously from the moment of registration.

questions.question: A member can enforce a provision of the constitution against the company if the provision:

options.A: Relates to their employment within the company
options.B: Confers a right on them in their capacity as a member (e.g., right to receive a dividend, right to attend meetings)
options.C: Names them specifically as an officer or agent
options.D: Confers a contractual benefit on any natural person

questions.explanation: Under the S31 statutory contract and the rule in Hickman v Kent, a member may enforce a constitutional provision against the company only where the right in question is a right conferred on them in their capacity as a member — such as the right to vote, attend meetings, or receive dividends.

questions.question: Can the company enforce the constitution against a member who refuses to comply with a valid call on shares?

options.A: No — the company has no contractual rights against members under S31
options.B: Yes — the constitution also binds members to the company, so the company may enforce constitutional obligations against them
options.C: Only if the court grants leave
options.D: No — the company must sue under the general law of contract

questions.explanation: The S31 statutory contract is mutual: it binds both members to the company and the company to members. This means the company may enforce constitutional obligations against members, such as the obligation to pay calls on shares.

questions.question: The constitution of a company can be altered by:

options.B: A special resolution of members (75% majority) under section 32 CA 2014

questions.explanation: Section 32 CA 2014 provides that a company may alter its constitution by special resolution — requiring a 75% majority of those voting. Subject to any limitations in the constitution itself, this power is wide and can cover most provisions.
